clips.title: A Tale of Unmet Needs and Broken Systems: Helene Recovery After 18 ...

clips.fullText:
AÂ year and a half ago, Hurricane HeleneÂ devastated WesternÂ North Carolina,Â marking the costliest storm that has ever hit NC and the deadliest storm in the contiguous U.S.Â since HurricaneÂ Katrina.Â Although the region hasÂ nowÂ transitioned to the long-term recovery phase, many ofÂ theÂ same human needsÂ that went neglected in the early monthsÂ following Helene remain unmetÂ as a result ofÂ bad policy choicesÂ and broken disaster recovery systems.Â Â 
With no new disaster recovery fundingÂ from the stateÂ since June, it is time for the NC General Assembly to step up andÂ make the investments that Western NC needs and strengthen the systems that allow communities to recover when a disaster strikes.Â Â 
Strained economies followingÂ aÂ disaster are not inevitableÂ 
When a disaster hits, businesses close and workers lose their jobs â€” all the more so in a region like Western NC that is heavily dependent on small businesses, which often donâ€™t have locations outside the affected region to help maintain revenue. How quickly the situation is stabilized and returns to normal levels is a function of the quality of our disaster recovery systems and the choices of policymakers.
By comparing the unemployment level in counties in Western NC with pre-Helene levels, we find that unemployment remains significantly elevated.

The lack of economic recovery in the region is the result of failing systems and poor policy choices.Â Â 
Unemployment benefits areÂ a key mechanism for stabilizing economiesÂ duringÂ a crisis. By providing workers who loseÂ their job with income, households canÂ maintainÂ spending, ensuring not only that they are able to pay their bills and meet their needs but also provideÂ revenue for businesses, who are then able to stay open andÂ maintainÂ their payrolls.Â Â 
However, both our state Unemployment Insurance (UI) and federal Disaster Unemployment Assistance (DUA) programsÂ pay outÂ farÂ too little in benefits for too short a duration and for too few workers.Â Â 
WithÂ state UI benefits capped atÂ 20 weeksÂ andÂ DUA benefits capped atÂ 26 weeks,Â 78 weeksÂ after Helene, unemployed disaster survivors who were eligible at all have long ago exhaustedÂ the meagerÂ assistanceÂ these programs provide. WithoutÂ robustÂ incomeÂ supports, survivors not only suffer,Â butÂ alsoÂ businessesÂ becauseÂ familiesÂ donâ€™tÂ have dollars to spend locally.Â Â 
WithÂ theÂ ongoing uncertainty and loansÂ businesses have taken onÂ following the COVID-19 pandemic,Â small businesses owners have been calling for revenue stabilizationÂ in the form ofÂ grants or forgivable loans.Â Â 
Early in 2025,Â Dogwood Health Trust, in collaboration withÂ Gov. JoshÂ Stein, provided $55 million in grants for small businesses,Â ultimatelyÂ supportingÂ over 2,100 businesses and helping toÂ retainÂ over 8,000 jobs.Â Yet the programÂ had over 7,350 applicants, meaning only about one-fourthÂ of applicants received a grant.Â Â 
In May, the NC House introducedÂ a billÂ thatÂ includedÂ more funding for small business grants, yet the NC Senate blocked these effortsÂ and ignored what small businesses said they need, instead throwing millions of dollars at tourismÂ ads.Â Â 
The way to enable a full economic recovery is funding small business grants tied to payroll retention.Â Alongside these grants, the NC General Assembly needs to use our robust UI trust fundÂ â€”Â second in the nation only to OregonÂ â€”Â toÂ strengthen our state UI program, includingÂ through the inclusion ofÂ expandedÂ assistanceÂ for workers who lose their jobÂ due toÂ a disaster.Â Â 
These changes should not be one-off fixes but developed into lasting systems that provideÂ allÂ North Carolinians with confidence that when a disaster strikes, mechanismsÂ areÂ in place to support them in recovery.Â Â 
Unmet housing needsÂ remainÂ a serious issueÂ for survivorsÂ 
Hurricane Helene destroyed and left tens of thousands of homes uninhabitable.Â Displaced homeowners found themselves having to juggle rent and a mortgage.Â With the loss of incomeÂ andÂ the rising cost of rent due toÂ landlords and property management companies taking advantage of theÂ supply shortage,Â tenants faced the threat of homelessness following waves of evictions.Â Â 
Fortunately,Â FEMAÂ announcedÂ last week that housing and rentalÂ assistanceÂ programs for Helene survivors have been extended for anÂ additionalÂ sixÂ months.Â ButÂ even setting asideÂ the demanding eligibility requirementsÂ andÂ the frequent improper denialsÂ for FEMA programs,Â severalÂ long-termÂ housing needsÂ remain.Â Â 
Long-term housing recovery is dominated by the U.S.Â Department of Housing and Urban Developmentâ€™s (HUD)Â Community Development Block Grant Disaster Recovery (CDBG-DR).Â AlthoughÂ CDBG-DRÂ moneyÂ isÂ flexibleÂ fundingÂ thatÂ can be used for any long-term unmet recovery needs, grant recipientsÂ often use the money as aÂ major source of funding for home rebuilding.Â Â 
For instance,Â NCÂ decided to spendÂ $1 billionÂ ofÂ theirÂ $1.4 billionÂ allocation on housing recovery. WhileÂ homes are finally being rebuiltÂ through theÂ Renew NC Program, a series of issuesÂ remain:Â Â 

RelocationÂ assistance:Â ToÂ maximize the spending onÂ home rebuilding,Â Renew NCÂ has chosen to offer no temporary relocationÂ assistanceÂ toÂ provide families with shelter or a place to store theirÂ possessionsÂ while their homes are rebuilt.Â As a result, families that would otherwise be eligibleÂ haveÂ pulledÂ out of the program orÂ did not botherÂ toÂ submitÂ an application at all.Â 

HotelÂ denials:Â Compounding the problem,Â many hotels have policies that prohibit residentsÂ with home addresses in theÂ sameÂ ZIPÂ code from booking a room, as well as prohibitingÂ guestsÂ from staying for more thanÂ 90 days. These policies mean thatÂ disaster survivors who could otherwise afford to rent these rooms are left without a place to stay.Â Â 

AccessibilityÂ andÂ inspectionÂ standards:Â In Eastern NC, the home rebuilding process is still ongoing from hurricanes Matthew (2016) and Florence (2018).Â YetÂ many of the homesÂ that have been completed face basic construction issues andÂ fail toÂ meet the accessibility needs of occupantsÂ despite passing inspection.Â Â 

InsufficientÂ funding:Â Excluding CDBG-DR funding, theÂ stateÂ has estimated a total ofÂ $5.3 billionÂ in unfunded residential damage in Western NC. That means thatÂ $1 billionÂ CDBG-DR allocation covers less than 20% of this unmet need,Â leaving tens of thousands of disaster survivors without a path to permanent housing.Â Â 

These problemsÂ shouldÂ be addressed promptly while we are still early in the home rebuilding process.Â NCÂ CommerceÂ hasÂ alreadyÂ receivedÂ $120 millionÂ from the NC General Assembly for home reconstruction and repair.Â This is money that is available and can be used by Renew NCÂ to provide temporary housingÂ assistance.Â Â 
This should not be a one-off fix butÂ a permanent solutionÂ so thatÂ we have the systems in place to support survivors forÂ futureÂ disasters, and addressing this systemic need must go hand in hand with thinking about how theseÂ temporary relocation services are going to be provided.Â Alongside this, there is an opportunity to craft a more robust inspection process that requiresÂ periodicÂ check-insÂ with homeowners present and a meaningful appeals process that includes the opportunity to address problems afterÂ move-in.Â Â 
Similarly, at the federal level, another round of CDBG-DR funding is needed so that more survivors canÂ rebuild their homes.Â But theÂ longer-termÂ systemic issue is one of statutorily authorizingÂ the CDBG-DR program so that Congress does not need to approve funds for each disaster, streamlining theÂ distribution of these essential funds,Â andÂ ensuring thatÂ recovery effortsÂ arenâ€™tÂ held hostage to the political agendas of those in power.Â Â 
Building aÂ state where all can thriveÂ 
NearlyÂ $45 billionÂ of the totalÂ $60Â billionÂ in estimatedÂ damageÂ remainsÂ unfunded,Â and these needs are not limited to housing andÂ the Western NC economy. ButÂ theyÂ are someÂ of the mostÂ essential andÂ neglectedÂ human-facing elementsÂ of recovery, and theyÂ serve as a powerful illustration ofÂ the need to develop moreÂ effective disasterÂ systems,Â especially withÂ disasters becoming more frequent and more severe.Â 
In the past,Â for storms of a comparable scale, the federal government funded anywhere from 32 percent to 78 percent of the total damage. So, withÂ less thanÂ 10% of the total damage caused by HeleneÂ funded by the federal government, the recovery effort out west also serves toÂ demonstrateÂ that building these systems requiresÂ coordination among state and federal governmentsÂ and that these systems cannot be built by states alone.Â Â 
But North Carolinians cannot afford to wait any longer on federal action. In the absence of sufficient federal support, we are relying on our stateâ€™s elected leaders to step up â€” and that requires making different choices.Â 
Our state lawmakers must move away from their relentless tax-cutting approach and instead invest in the needs of our communities, from WesternÂ NCÂ to EasternÂ NC. Disaster survivors should not have to wait a decade for their homes to be rebuilt, only to discover those homes are falling apart upon completion or that they will never be rebuilt at all. Workers and small businesses should not be left to fend for themselves while they recover, because that hurts us all.Â 
North Carolina is a state rich in resources. Our lawmakers must stop siphoning those resources to big corporations and the ultra-wealthy while families and communities struggle to make ends meet. We need investments in systems that allow all North Carolinians not just to get by, but to recover and thrive.
